content.text: Why New Account Security Matters

A freshly created AWS account is simultaneously a blank slate and a target. Attackers actively scan for credentials exposed in public repositories, default configurations left unchanged, and accounts with open billing. AWS accounts have been compromised within minutes of their access keys being accidentally committed to a public GitHub repository — the attacker's automation is faster than a human can react.

Securing a new account is not a one-time task — it is establishing a baseline that all future work builds on. The controls you put in place in the first week determine whether security is built in from the start or retrofitted after an incident. Retrofitting is always more expensive.

The approach here organizes controls into three layers: Identity and Access (who can do what), Detection and Visibility (seeing what is happening), and Protective Controls (reducing the attack surface). All three layers operate concurrently — each compensates for gaps in the others.
The Three Security Layers

attrs.alt: Three-layer AWS account security model: Layer 1 Identity (root MFA, IAM admin, password policy, delete root keys, alternate contacts), Layer 2 Detection (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security Hub, billing alerts), Layer 3 Protection (S3 block public access, AWS Config, IAM Access Analyzer, default VPC review)

content.text: The diagram maps each security control to its layer and implementation priority. Day 0 actions are non-negotiable — the account is insecure until they are done. Week 1 actions establish ongoing visibility and detection. Week 2 actions add deeper configuration auditing and access validation. Together, they build a layered defense where failure of one control does not create a critical gap.
Layer 1: Identity and Access Control
Step 1: Enable Root MFA (Day 0)

The root user is the most powerful identity in an AWS account and the highest-value target for attackers. Without MFA, anyone who obtains the root email and password has full, unrestricted account access. MFA makes the password alone insufficient — a second physical factor is required.
Sign in to the console as root
Click your account name (top right) → Security credentials
Under Multi-factor authentication → Assign MFA device
Choose FIDO2 security key (recommended) or Virtual MFA device
Complete the enrollment flow and name the device clearly (e.g., root-yubikey-primary)
Enroll a second MFA device as backup under a different name

# Verify root MFA is enabled (run as an IAM user, not root)
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled' --output text
# Expected: 1

# List registered MFA devices for root (virtual only; hardware keys listed differently)
aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices --assignment-status Assigned \
  --query 'VirtualMFADevices[?contains(User.Arn, `:root`)].SerialNumber'
Step 2: Delete Root Access Keys (Day 0)

Root access keys are the single most dangerous credential type in AWS — they grant full programmatic access to everything with no policy restriction. New accounts do not have root access keys by default, but checking and confirming their absence is part of every security baseline.

# Check for root access keys
aws iam get-account-summary \
  --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent' --output text
# Expected: 0

# If non-zero: sign in as root → Security credentials → Access keys → Delete all
# Never create root access keys for automation — use IAM roles instead
Step 3: Create an IAM Admin User and Stop Using Root (Day 0)

After securing the root user, create an IAM user (or configure IAM Identity Center) for all day-to-day administrative work. The root user should be locked away and used only for root-only tasks — closing the account, changing the root email, enabling IAM billing access, and a handful of others.

# Create an IAM admin user with console access
aws iam create-user --user-name admin-yourname

# Attach AdministratorAccess policy
aws iam attach-user-policy \
  --user-name admin-yourname \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Create a login profile (console password)
aws iam create-login-profile \
  --user-name admin-yourname \
  --password 'TemporaryPass1!' \
  --password-reset-required

# The user will be prompted to change password at first login
# After creating the IAM user: log out as root, log in as IAM user, set up IAM user MFA

Consider using IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) instead of IAM users for any account that is part of an organization or where multiple people need access. Identity Center integrates with corporate identity providers and avoids creating long-lived IAM credentials for humans.
Step 4: Set an IAM Account Password Policy (Day 0)
The account-level password policy governs all IAM user passwords. AWS has weak defaults; explicitly set a strong policy:

# Set a strong account password policy
aws iam update-account-password-policy \
  --minimum-password-length 14 \
  --require-symbols \
  --require-numbers \
  --require-uppercase-characters \
  --require-lowercase-characters \
  --allow-users-to-change-password \
  --max-password-age 90 \
  --password-reuse-prevention 24 \
  --hard-expiry false

# Verify the policy was applied
aws iam get-account-password-policy
Step 5: Configure Alternate Contacts (Day 0)
AWS routes security alerts, billing notifications, and operational events to the account root email by default. Setting alternate contacts ensures the right teams receive these messages:

# Set security contact (receives vulnerability disclosures and security findings)
aws account put-alternate-contact \
  --alternate-contact-type SECURITY \
  --name "Security Team" \
  --email-address security@yourcompany.com \
  --phone-number "+1-555-0100" \
  --title "Security Operations"

# Set billing contact
aws account put-alternate-contact \
  --alternate-contact-type BILLING \
  --name "Finance Team" \
  --email-address billing@yourcompany.com \
  --phone-number "+1-555-0101" \
  --title "Finance Director"

# Set operations contact
aws account put-alternate-contact \
  --alternate-contact-type OPERATIONS \
  --name "Platform Team" \
  --email-address platform-ops@yourcompany.com \
  --phone-number "+1-555-0102" \
  --title "Platform Engineer"
Layer 2: Detection and Visibility
Step 6: Enable CloudTrail in All Regions (Week 1)

CloudTrail records every API call made in your account — who called what, from where, and when. Without CloudTrail, you have no audit trail if something goes wrong and no way to investigate the sequence of events after an incident.

A multi-region trail is the correct configuration for a new account: it captures events from all regions, including regions you are not actively using (where attackers sometimes operate to avoid detection).

# Create an S3 bucket for CloudTrail logs (bucket names must be globally unique)
ACCOUNT_ID=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)
BUCKET="cloudtrail-logs-${ACCOUNT_ID}"

aws s3 mb s3://${BUCKET} --region us-east-1

# Apply a bucket policy that allows CloudTrail to write logs
cat > /tmp/cloudtrail-bucket-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "CloudTrailAclCheck",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "cloudtrail.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "s3:GetBucketAcl",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CloudTrailWrite",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "cloudtrail.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "s3:PutObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/AWSLogs/ACCOUNT_ID/*",
      "Condition": {"StringEquals": {"s3:x-amz-acl": "bucket-owner-full-control"}}
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Create a multi-region trail
aws cloudtrail create-trail \
  --name management-events \
  --s3-bucket-name ${BUCKET} \
  --is-multi-region-trail \
  --enable-log-file-validation \
  --include-global-service-events

aws cloudtrail start-logging --name management-events

# Verify the trail is active
aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name management-events \
  --query '{Logging:IsLogging, LastDelivery:LatestDeliveryTime}'
Step 7: Enable GuardDuty (Week 1)

GuardDuty is AWS's managed threat detection service. It analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS query logs for signs of malicious behavior — compromised credentials, crypto mining, data exfiltration, port scanning, and more. The cost is low (typically $3-$10/month for a new account) and the threat model coverage is extensive.

# Enable GuardDuty in your primary region
aws guardduty create-detector \
  --enable \
  --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES

# Enable GuardDuty in additional regions (repeat for each region you use)
for REGION in us-west-2 eu-west-1 ap-southeast-1; do
  echo "Enabling GuardDuty in ${REGION}"
  aws guardduty create-detector \
    --enable \
    --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES \
    --region ${REGION}
done

# Verify GuardDuty is active
aws guardduty list-detectors \
  --query 'DetectorIds'

GuardDuty produces findings rather than blocking actions. A finding is an alert that describes a detected threat, including the affected resource, the threat type, and the evidence. You should route findings to an SNS topic or Slack channel so they are actioned promptly.
Step 8: Enable Security Hub (Week 1)

Security Hub aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, IAM Access Analyzer, Inspector, and other services into a single dashboard. It also continuously evaluates your account against the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices (FSBP) standard and the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark, flagging deviations.

# Enable Security Hub with default standards
aws securityhub enable-security-hub --enable-default-standards

# The default standards enabled:
# - AWS Foundational Security Best Practices (FSBP)
# - CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark

# Get a summary of Security Hub findings by severity
aws securityhub get-findings \
  --filters '{"SeverityLabel":[{"Value":"CRITICAL","Comparison":"EQUALS"},{"Value":"HIGH","Comparison":"EQUALS"}]}' \
  --query 'Findings[].{Title:Title, Severity:Severity.Label, Status:Compliance.Status}' \
  --max-items 20 \
  --output table
Step 9: Enable Billing Alerts (Day 0)

Billing alerts notify you before unexpected charges accumulate into a large invoice. AWS does not send billing alerts by default — you must enable them. The two-step process requires enabling billing alerts in the Billing preferences, then creating a CloudWatch alarm.

# Step 1: Enable billing alerts (requires root or billing permissions)
# This must be done in the console: Billing → Billing preferences → Receive Billing Alerts

# Step 2: Create a CloudWatch alarm on EstimatedCharges
# Note: billing metrics only exist in us-east-1
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name monthly-charges-exceed-5-dollars \
  --metric-name EstimatedCharges \
  --namespace AWS/Billing \
  --statistic Maximum \
  --period 86400 \
  --threshold 5.00 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:ACCOUNT_ID:billing-alerts \
  --dimensions Name=Currency,Value=USD \
  --region us-east-1

# Also create an AWS Budget for a monthly spending threshold
aws budgets create-budget \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text) \
  --budget '{
    "BudgetName": "monthly-cost-limit",
    "BudgetLimit": {"Amount": "50", "Unit": "USD"},
    "TimeUnit": "MONTHLY",
    "BudgetType": "COST"
  }' \
  --notifications-with-subscribers '[{
    "Notification": {
      "NotificationType": "ACTUAL",
      "ComparisonOperator": "GREATER_THAN",
      "Threshold": 80
    },
    "Subscribers": [{"SubscriptionType": "EMAIL", "Address": "you@example.com"}]
  }]'
Layer 3: Protective Controls
Step 10: Enable S3 Block Public Access at Account Level (Day 0)

S3 bucket misconfigurations that accidentally expose data publicly are one of the most common causes of AWS data breaches. Block Public Access at the account level is a single setting that prevents any S3 bucket in the account from becoming publicly accessible — even if a misconfigured bucket policy or ACL would otherwise allow it.

# Block all public access at the account level
aws s3control put-public-access-block \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text) \
  --public-access-block-configuration \
  BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

# Verify the setting
aws s3control get-public-access-block \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)

If you legitimately need a public S3 bucket (static website hosting, public downloads), you can override this setting per-bucket after explicitly disabling the account-level block for that specific bucket. The account-level block does not prevent legitimate public hosting — it prevents accidental exposure.
Step 11: Enable IAM Access Analyzer (Week 1)

IAM Access Analyzer identifies resources that are shared with external AWS accounts or the public — S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda functions, SQS queues, and Secrets Manager secrets. It creates a finding whenever a resource policy grants access to an entity outside your account or organization trust zone.

# Create an analyzer for your account
aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer \
  --analyzer-name account-access-analyzer \
  --type ACCOUNT

# If using AWS Organizations, use ORGANIZATION type for broader scope:
# aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer --analyzer-name org-analyzer --type ORGANIZATION

# List any existing findings (unintended external access)
aws accessanalyzer list-findings \
  --analyzer-arn $(aws accessanalyzer list-analyzers \
    --query 'analyzers[0].arn' --output text) \
  --query 'findings[].{Resource:resource, Principal:principal, Status:status}' \
  --output table
Step 12: Review the Default VPC (Week 1)

Every AWS region comes with a default VPC that is pre-configured with public subnets, an internet gateway, and security groups that allow all inbound traffic. This makes it easy to get started but creates a security risk: any EC2 instance launched into the default VPC is internet-accessible unless you explicitly restrict it.
The secure approach depends on your use case:
Action
When Appropriate
How
Delete the default VPC entirely
Accounts where all networking is custom
VPC console → Default VPC → Delete; or aws ec2 delete-vpc
Keep and harden the default VPC
Learning accounts, quick experiments
Remove the 0.0.0.0/0 inbound rule from default security group
Create a custom VPC for production
Production workloads
Use a private subnet, NAT Gateway, and strict security groups

# Check the default VPC's security group rules (look for 0.0.0.0/0 inbound)
DEFAULT_VPC=$(aws ec2 describe-vpcs \
  --filters Name=isDefault,Values=true \
  --query 'Vpcs[0].VpcId' --output text)

aws ec2 describe-security-groups \
  --filters Name=vpc-id,Values=${DEFAULT_VPC} Name=group-name,Values=default \
  --query 'SecurityGroups[].IpPermissions'

# Remove the permissive inbound rule from the default security group
# (get the security group ID first)
DEFAULT_SG=$(aws ec2 describe-security-groups \
  --filters Name=vpc-id,Values=${DEFAULT_VPC} Name=group-name,Values=default \
  --query 'SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)

# Revoke all inbound rules from the default security group
aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id ${DEFAULT_SG} \
  --ip-permissions '[{"IpProtocol":"-1","IpRanges":[{"CidrIp":"0.0.0.0/0"}]}]'
Security Baseline Verification Script
Run this script after completing the initial setup to verify the core security controls are in place. It checks the most critical settings and reports pass/fail for each:

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== AWS Account Security Baseline Check ==="
PASS=0
FAIL=0
ACCOUNT_ID=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)

check() {
  local desc="$1"
  local result="$2"
  local expected="$3"
  if [ "$result" = "$expected" ]; then
    echo "PASS: $desc"
    PASS=$((PASS + 1))
  else
    echo "FAIL: $desc (got: $result, expected: $expected)"
    FAIL=$((FAIL + 1))
  fi
}

# 1. Root MFA
MFA=$(aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled' --output text)
check "Root MFA enabled" "$MFA" "1"

# 2. No root access keys
KEYS=$(aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent' --output text)
check "Root access keys absent" "$KEYS" "0"

# 3. S3 Block Public Access
BPA=$(aws s3control get-public-access-block --account-id ${ACCOUNT_ID} \
  --query 'PublicAccessBlockConfiguration.BlockPublicAcls' --output text 2>/dev/null)
check "S3 Block Public Access enabled" "$BPA" "True"

# 4. CloudTrail active
CT=$(aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name management-events \
  --query 'IsLogging' --output text 2>/dev/null)
check "CloudTrail logging active" "$CT" "True"

# 5. GuardDuty enabled
GD=$(aws guardduty list-detectors --query 'length(DetectorIds)' --output text)
check "GuardDuty enabled in this region" "$GD" "1"

echo ""
echo "Results: ${PASS} passed, ${FAIL} failed"
if [ $FAIL -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "Address failed checks before deploying any production workloads."
fi
Ongoing Security Hygiene
Account security is not a one-time event. After the initial baseline is set, establish these recurring practices:
Cadence
Practice
Tool
Daily
Review GuardDuty findings and Security Hub alerts
GuardDuty console or EventBridge alerts
Weekly
Check AWS Health for service events
Health Dashboard or API
Monthly
Review IAM Access Analyzer findings
Access Analyzer console
Monthly
Run Trusted Advisor (Business+ plan)
Trusted Advisor console or API
Quarterly
Review IAM users, roles, and unused permissions
IAM console + Access Advisor
Quarterly
Rotate IAM access keys older than 90 days
IAM credential report
Annually
Rotate root password, review MFA devices
Root console login
Annually
Well-Architected Review of security pillar
AWS Well-Architected Tool

Security Hub's automated compliance checks run continuously and surface new issues as your account evolves. Checking the Security Hub dashboard once a week takes 5 minutes and catches configuration drift before it becomes a vulnerability.
Summary

Securing a new AWS account requires action across three layers: Identity (root MFA, IAM admin user, password policy, no root access keys, alternate contacts), Detection (CloudTrail in all regions, GuardDuty for threat detection, Security Hub for compliance posture, billing alerts), and Protection (S3 Block Public Access at account level, IAM Access Analyzer for external access findings, default VPC hardening).

Day 0 actions — root MFA, no root access keys, IAM admin user, S3 Block Public Access, billing alerts, and alternate contacts — take less than 30 minutes and eliminate the most common attack vectors for new accounts. Week 1 actions add the detection and visibility layer. The verification script confirms the baseline is correctly established before any production workload is deployed.
